title_en: Sawm and Hajj: Fasting and Pilgrimage in Shafi'i Fiqh
content_en:
Ash-Shafi'i's discussions of fasting (sawm) and Hajj in Al-Umm share the same methodological commitment: rulings are derived from the Quran and authenticated Sunnah, and scholarly disagreements are resolved by returning to the prophetic narrations rather than local custom or juristic preference.

The Ramadan fast is obligatory on every sane, adult, resident Muslim who is physically capable. The Shafi'i school requires a fresh intention each night before Fajr for each day of Ramadan — a single intention at the month's start does not suffice. The fast runs from the first light of Fajr until sunset, during which time the fasting person abstains from eating, drinking, and sexual intercourse. Ash-Shafi'i identifies deliberate vomiting and the introduction of any substance into the stomach through any body opening as additional nullifiers.

Forgetful eating or drinking does not break the fast, based on the authenticated hadith of the Prophet in Bukhari. The Shafi'i school holds that the fast is not broken by tasting food that does not reach the throat, though deliberately swallowing anything does break it. The school is also known for its strict position that a kiss that leads to ejaculation breaks the fast, while a kiss without such outcome does not.

For those excused from fasting, the Shafi'i school distinguishes clearly: the temporarily sick or traveling person makes up the missed days later (qada'); the permanently incapable elderly person pays fidyah (feeding one poor person per day) in lieu of fasting. Pregnant and nursing women who fear harm may break the fast and must make up the days — and additionally pay fidyah — if the fear was for the child rather than themselves alone.

For Hajj, ash-Shafi'i holds it obligatory once in a lifetime for those with the means, and holds Umrah equally obligatory — a position shared with the Hanbali school but not the Hanafi or Maliki schools. The Shafi'i school identifies the essential pillars of Hajj as ihram with intention, standing at Arafah, tawaf al-ifadah, and sa'y. Without any of these, the Hajj is invalid and cannot be compensated by sacrifice.

The prohibitions of ihram receive careful treatment: no cutting of hair or nails, no perfume, no hunting, no marriage contracts, no sexual relations, and for men, no stitched garments or head coverings. Expiations for violations follow the Quranic provision of fasting, charity, or sacrifice (2:196), with detailed rules for which expiation applies to which violation.

The farewell tawaf is obligatory for all who performed Hajj, except menstruating and post-natal women who are excused on the authority of the Prophet's explicit permission narrated in Bukhari and Muslim. One who leaves without the farewell tawaf without excuse owes a compensatory sacrifice.

Ash-Shafi'i's discussion of Hajj is enriched by his personal experience of performing it multiple times, and his first-hand encounter with the scholarly practices of both Madinah and Makkah — the two sacred cities whose traditions he synthesized in his Egyptian jurisprudence.
title_id: Shaum dan Haji: Puasa dan Ibadah Haji dalam Fikih Syafi'i
content_id:
Dua pilar terakhir Islam — puasa Ramadan dan haji ke Baitullah — dibahas secara mendalam dalam Al-Umm. Al-Syafi'i menyajikannya dengan metodologi yang sama: dalil dari Al-Quran, hadis sahih, kemudian analisis dan istinbath hukum.

Pembahasan puasa mencakup: wajibnya puasa Ramadan dan penetapan awal bulan (al-Syafi'i menerima rukyah dengan satu saksi yang adil), niat puasa, hal-hal yang membatalkan puasa, kafarat hubungan suami-istri di siang hari Ramadan, golongan yang mendapat keringanan (musafir, sakit, hamil, menyusui), qadha, dan puasa sunnah.

Pembahasan haji mencakup: wajibnya haji sekali seumur hidup, istitha'ah (kemampuan) sebagai syarat, jenis-jenis haji, miqat-miqat, ihram dan larangan-larangannya, thawaf, sa'i, wukuf di Arafah, melempar jumrah, hadyu dan udhiyah, tata urutan haji yang sahih menurut al-Syafi'i, dan berbagai dam serta kafaratnya.
content_ar:
تشترك مباحث الصوم والحج في الأم في الالتزام المنهجي ذاته: استنباط الأحكام من القرآن والسنة الصحيحة، وبيان الخلاف العلمي مع توثيق الأدلة، دون تنازل عن المنهج النصي الذي يُميّز الشافعي.

وفي الصوم يُبيّن الشافعي شروط صحته وأحكام المفطرات ومسائل ثبوت الهلال والقضاء والكفارة. ومن خصائص موقفه: قبول شاهد واحد في ثبوت رمضان وهو ما يفارق به الحنابلة المشترطين لشاهدَين، مستنداً إلى أحاديث بعينها.

وفي الحج يُعرض الشافعي أنواعه الثلاثة والتفاضل بينها، وكيفية أداء مناسكه من الإحرام والطواف والسعي والوقوف والرمي والحلق والطواف. ومن مواقفه المميزة: اشتراط الموالاة في أشواط السعي وعدم التفريق الطويل بينها.
